narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an LSAT score of 33, you've put together a respectable academic profile, including full-time enrollment plans and a lower-middle family income background that reflects determination to pursue law despite not coming from significant wealth. The university's admission model, trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try, estimated your probability of failing at 50.8%—just over the tipping point that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass reliably. This close call means the model saw you as a moderate risk, not a sure bet, based on how your details stacked up against thousands of similar applicants. The most influential factor pulling in your favor was your undergraduate GPA, which stands above the typical 3.21 average and signaled to the model stronger preparation than many peers, nudging the prediction toward expecting you to pass. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—well below the average of 5.53—emerged as the top driver toward failure, suggesting later academic struggles that outweighed your earlier potential in the model's eyes. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also lagging behind the 5.71 average, added further concern by reinforcing a pattern of underperformance relative to classmates. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, where higher deciles and balanced records dominate, your profile showed these dips as red flags, even with your above-average undergrad record providing some counterbalance. While your LSAT was a bit below the 36.7 average and family income lower than most, they played smaller roles here. This isn't a judgment on your character or potential as a future lawyer—it's the model highlighting patterns from past data where similar profiles faced bar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to push closer to or above average, bolstering recent grades through post-bac courses or targeted prep to improve those decile perceptions, and applying to schools with more holistic reviews that might weigh your full-time commitment and resilience more favorably. Gathering strong recommendation letters emphasizing your growth could also help reframe your story for the next cycle. With focused improvements, you can shift the odds and find the right path to law school.
